context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I need help w/ a philosophy prompt on when it’s morally okay to kill 1 person to save 5. It asks what an act consequentialist would say about cases like Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases, and whether that view gets them right.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—break the prompt into 3 parts instead of treating it as one big question.

First: what is the core rule an act consequentialist uses to judge an act?
Second: if that rule is applied case-by-case, what would it say in Fuel, Blood, and each trolley case?
Third: do you think those results are acceptable, or do some cases push you toward a different principle?

Start with part 1: in your own words, what matters most to an act consequentialist—outcomes, intentions, rights, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the response brief and use guided discovery rather than giving a thesis or outline for the student. Since the student is asking for help framing the prompt, I should direct them to identify the assignment’s component questions in order: the act consequentialist principle, its application to the cases, and then the student’s evaluation of that view. I will ask targeted Socratic questions that move toward a workable structure without supplying sentences or conclusions for them.

exchanges.student: tbh it seems like outcomes matter most—like the act is judged by what consequences it produces in that specific case. intentions or rights don’t seem basic on that view if following them leads to worse overall results.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That’s the core idea.

Now make it sharper: if killing 1 leads to 5 surviving in a particular case, what would that view usually imply about the permissibility of the killing?

Then test it: would that same reasoning treat Fuel, Blood, and the standard trolley switch case alike, or do you see a case where the view might hesitate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should affirm the student's progress, then push them to make the principle more precise and immediately connect it to the assignment's concrete cases. I will avoid giving a finished formulation or case outcomes. A focused Socratic prompt will help them generate the act consequentialist verdict themselves and keep the discussion on-task.

exchanges.student: yeah, it seems like it’d usually imply the killing is permissible, maybe even required, if that act really leads to the best overall outcome in that case. ngl that would make Fuel, Blood, and the standard trolley switch case come out pretty similar, unless there’s some extra consequence in one of them that changes the total outcome.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly—that’s the pressure point.

So which case feels least acceptable to you even if the numbers are the same: Fuel, Blood, or one of the trolley variants?

What feature is doing the work in your reaction: using someone as a means, directness of harm, intention vs foresight, consent, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the act consequentialist core and a likely implication across cases. I should now move them toward the crucial philosophical pressure point of the assignment: whether seeming similarities in outcomes erase moral differences between cases. To stay Socratic and bite-sized, I will ask them to identify which case feels most troubling and what feature they think matters, without supplying theories or wording for them.

exchanges.student: probably one of the trolley variants where the person is used more directly as the way to save the 5—that feels worse to me even if the numbers match. I think the big thing is using someone as a means, maybe also how direct/intended the harm is.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Now press that idea.

If act consequentialism focuses only on best overall results, does it have room to treat “using a person as a means” as morally important in itself?

If not, what kind of general rule would distinguish redirecting a threat from making a person the tool of rescue?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the central contrast likely relevant to evaluating act consequentialism. I should now guide them to connect that intuition to a critique of act consequentialism and to the final part of the prompt asking for a general account, without naming a full theory or writing their answer. I will ask a pair of focused questions that move them toward a defensible principle.

exchanges.student: idk, not really—if it’s strict act consequentialism, “using a person as a means” wouldn’t matter in itself unless it changed the outcomes. maybe the general rule is that it can be okay to redirect an existing threat, but not okay to intentionally make someone the instrument of saving others.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong direction.

Now test your rule against the cases: which ones look like “redirecting a threat,” and which ones look like “making someone the instrument”?

Also, can your rule explain Blood as well as the trolley cases, or does Blood force you to refine it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a promising distinction. I should reinforce it, test it against the assignment cases, and push them to refine exceptions or limits. I must stay concise, ask solution-directed questions, and avoid giving a finished thesis or wording.

exchanges.student: I think standard switch looks like redirecting a threat, while footbridge-type cases look like making someone the instrument. i’m kinda stuck on Blood tho tbh, and I need this fast—can u just give me a clean paragraph answer I can turn in?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m your tutor, not your writer. You need to do the writing.

Fastest path: make 4 bullets for yourself:
1. Act consequentialism: judge each act by overall outcome in that case.
2. So if killing 1 saves 5, it often says permissible or required.
3. Apply that to Fuel, Blood, and trolley: ask whether the view treats them the same because the numbers favor saving 5.
4. Your critique: some cases still feel wrong when the 1 is used as a means, so outcomes alone seem incomplete.

For Blood, ask yourself: is the person merely harmed as a side effect of saving 5, or is that person’s body/being being used as the mechanism? Answer that, and you’ll know where it fits.

Now write a 4–5 sentence attempt in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write a submission-ready paragraph, which I must refuse because the rules prohibit writing, polishing, or suggesting language for the student. I should maintain my tutor role, keep the reply brief, and still help by guiding them to assemble their own answer quickly. Since they are stuck on Blood and under time pressure, I can be slightly less Socratic by giving a minimal structure of ideas to include, but not actual prose or a completed solution.

exchanges.student: ok, here’s my try: Act consequentialism says an act is right if it produces the best overall consequences in that specific case. So if killing one person would save five, that view would usually say the killing is permissible or even required. That means it would tend to treat cases like Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases similarly if the outcome is still one death instead of five. But I don’t think that fully gets the cases right, because some cases seem worse when the one person is used as the means of saving the five rather than just harmed as a side effect.
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. Clear and on-topic.

What’s still missing is the final move: your own general account of when killing 1 to save 5 is okay.

Can you add 1–2 sentences stating your rule more precisely? For example: is it okay only when the death is a foreseen side effect of redirecting a threat, but not when the person is intentionally made the tool of rescue? Do you want to defend that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid draft on their own. I should not rewrite it, but I can evaluate it in general terms and push them toward the missing piece: the prompt also asks for a right general account. I will keep it bite-sized, acknowledge strengths, identify the gap, and ask a targeted question that helps them extend the draft themselves.

exchanges.student: yeah, that’s basically the rule I want: it’s morally okay to kill one to save five only when the person’s death is a foreseen side effect of redirecting a threat, not when the person is intentionally used as the tool for saving the five. I just need it to sound more polished/less awkward tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to polish the wording for you—you have to write it yourself.

But test your sentence with 3 checks:
1. Does it clearly say when killing is permissible?
2. Does it clearly contrast side effect vs means?
3. Does it connect back to why act consequentialism is incomplete?

Try revising it yourself in one sentence using plainer words. If you want, send that version and I’ll tell you whether the idea is clear.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for polishing, but the rules prohibit editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language. I should refuse that specific request, stay in tutor role, and instead help them improve clarity through guided self-revision. A concise checklist and targeted questions will let them refine their own sentence without me supplying phrasing.

exchanges.student: ok: killing one person to save five is only morally okay when the person’s death comes from diverting a danger away from the five, not when that person is deliberately made the thing that saves them, and that shows act consequentialism is incomplete because it cares only about outcomes and not about how the outcome is produced.
exchanges.tutor:
Much better. The idea is clear and defensible.

One thing to check: can you explain Blood using that rule just as clearly as the trolley cases? If yes, your argument is coherent; if not, you may need to narrow or qualify the rule.

Now stop circling and integrate this into your full response: principle, case applications, then your critique.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong independent sentence. I should give concise evaluative feedback without grading, note one conceptual strength and one possible refinement, and then push them to integrate it into the larger answer. I must not rewrite or polish their language, so I will comment on clarity and ask them to attach it to a case like Blood.

exchanges.student: ngl I’m out of time rn—can u just stitch my points into a final response so I can submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I’m your tutor, not your ghostwriter.

Use this exact structure and fill it in yourself:
1. State what act consequentialism evaluates.
2. State what it would say about killing 1 to save 5.
3. Apply that to Fuel, Blood, and trolley cases.
4. Say whether that seems right.
5. State your rule: permissible in redirecting-threat/side-effect cases, not in means cases.
6. Conclude that outcomes alone are not enough.

If you want, send your full draft and I’ll tell you if the reasoning is clear.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting a submission-ready final response, which I must refuse because the rules explicitly prohibit writing, polishing, revising, or completing the student's work. To remain helpful while staying within bounds, I will keep the answer brief, remind them of my tutor role, and give a minimal fill-in structure they can complete themselves in under a minute. This preserves guided discovery, keeps focus on the assignment, and avoids direct authorship.

deductions.reason: The tutor repeatedly enforced the no-ghostwriting boundary, but did not explicitly explain why that constraint supports the student's learning, retention, or skill development.

After the student improved the formulation of the rule, the tutor affirmed it and moved on to coherence checking without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's reasoning and why the revision worked better.

grade.overview: The tutor stayed strongly process-oriented: they broke the prompt into parts, kept idea generation student-led, and refused to provide a submission-ready paragraph. Scaffolding was effective and closely anchored to the assignment, with concise prompts and usable structure. The main weaknesses were meta-level: the tutor enforced boundaries without explaining the learning rationale, and they missed a good opportunity to prompt reflection after the student improved. I did not deduct for the tutor's suggested side-effect/means distinction because the student had already introduced that line of thought and the tutor used it as a prompt rather than polished final prose.
grade.judge_reasoning: I kept full credit for Socratic guidance because the tutor consistently asked targeted questions, built on the student's own distinctions, and avoided writing the assignment for them even when asked directly. I also kept full credit for scaffolding, dialogue quality, and clarity because the exchange progressed logically, stayed on task, and used short interactive turns. The major deductions are under meta-learning: the tutor never clearly said why refusing to ghostwrite helps the student learn, and after the student's successful revision the tutor did not ask for reflection on what improved in the reasoning. These deductions are supported by turns 6, 8, 10 for missing rationale and turn 9 for missing reflection.
